titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the John Ashbery Collection of Artwork and Objects
Ashbery (John) Collection of Artwork and Objects
titlestmt.author: by Michael Rush, Karin Roffman, and Nancy Kuhl

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday June 1, 2022 at 06:07

did.unittitle: John Ashbery collection of artwork and objects

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The collection consists of artwork and objects collected by American poet John Ashbery. It includes paintings, glassware, ceramics, clothing, household objects, realia, printed material, collage materials, and other items of significance to Ashbery, some of which inspired his poetry or appeared in his published work. The collection includes materials by or received from Ashbery's husband David Kermani and artists Joe Brainard and Trevor Winkfield, among others. All materials were kept in Ashbery's house in Hudson, New York, or his New York City apartment.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: The collection consists of artwork and objects collected by American poet John Ashbery. It includes paintings, glassware, ceramics, clothing, household objects, realia, printed material, collage materials, and other items of significance to Ashbery, some of which inspired his poetry or appeared in his published work. The collection includes materials by or received from Ashbery's husband David Kermani and artists Joe Brainard and Trevor Winkfield, among others. All materials were kept in Ashbery's house in Hudson, New York, or his New York City apartment.

In close consultation with John Ashbery's husband David Kermani, curator Nancy Kuhl and Ashbery biographer Karin Roffman chose the objects in this collection from among many similar objects in John Ashbery's homes in New York City and Hudson, New York, shortly after his death. As representative samples from the poet's large and rich personal collections, the objects here suggest something of the wide range of Ashbery's interests and gesture towards the depth, complexity, and enormity of his collections in their entirety. As a subset of a much larger whole, this group highlights Ashbery's sensibility, an artist who collected both within and across categories (plates printed with rebus puzzles, for example, are at home in a larger collection of plates and a collection of rebus puzzles in various formats). Other objects from Ashbery's collections are held by libraries, museums, and institutions, including the Houghton Library at Harvard University, the Berg Collection of the New York Public Library, the Morgan Library and Museum, Deerfield Academy, the Pultneyville Historical Society, and the Flow Chart Foundation. The Beinecke Library's collection of Ashbery objects is organized both by place (New York City or Hudson) and by room, reflecting the location of objects at the time of Ashbery's death and for the preceding few years when decreased mobility inhibited the poet's practice of frequently moving individual objects from one room to another and from one home to another.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of the Estate of John Ashbery and David Kermani, 2018-2021.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two series: I. Artwork and objects from Ashbery's New York City apartment, 1819-2018, undated. II. Artwork and objects from Ashbery's Hudson, New York house, 1937-1995, undated.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections as they are acquired, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
This collection received a basic level of processing, including rehousing and minimal organization.

Information included in the Description of Papers note and Collection Contents section is drawn from information supplied with the collection and from an initial survey of the contents. Folder titles appearing in the contents list below are often based on those provided by the creator or previous custodian. Titles have not been verified against the contents of the folders in all cases. Otherwise, folder titles are supplied by staff during initial processing.
This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The John Ashbery Collection of Artwork and Objects is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: John Ashbery Collection of Artwork and Objects. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: John Ashbery Papers (MS Am 3189). Houghton Library, Harvard University, Cambridge, Massachusetts.

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: Many of the items included in this collection appear or are described in John Ashbery's Nest, a virtual tour of Ashbery's Hudson, New York home, which was developed in a collaboration between Ashbery biographer Karin Roffman and designers, photographers, and coders from the Yale University Digital Humanities Lab, Yale ITS, and VR Habitat, Inc.

did.unittitle: Artwork and objects from Ashbery's New York City apartment

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The artwork and objects from John Ashbery's New York City apartment are organized by room in sequence through the apartment: entryway, second bedroom / office, living room, bedroom, kitchen, and study.

did.unittitle: Six French rebus plates, including one from the Paris Exposition of 1819
Oval-shaped black and white ceramic box with papers

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Accompanied by two glass rings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Woodcut, artist's proof #14/25, 5 3/4 x 5 1/2 inches, signed and framed. Printed by John Erikson and published by the Cooper Union, New York.
This work was reproduced as the cover of Ashbery's 2002 collection Chinese Whispers .

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Unframed by Beinecke Library staff, November 2021.

did.unittitle: Photographs of objects in situ in entryway

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Unframed by Beinecke Library staff, November 2021.

did.unittitle: "The Punctuator" volvelle inscribed to Ashbery from [Alan?]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This item was behind the collage by Pierre Martory.

did.unittitle: Set of three cut paper silhouettes

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Collage.

did.unittitle: Photograph of note on the back of David Lehman's collage

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unframed, 18 x 3 3/4 inches.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oil on canvas, 12 x 12 inches. Signed, dated, titled and dedicated to Ashbery on back.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The contents include matches, playing cards, tarot cards, pipe cleaners, coasters, clippings, incense, a film canister, and other miscellaneous objects.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: John Wells, a friend of John Ashbery and David Kermani who sometimes worked for them, made many small works of trees, orobouros and other shapes while staying in their apartment. Most are either watercolor on paper or gouache on paper. Most were gifts and were made in the mid to late 1990s. A few are signed on the back.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Accompanied by a note from David Kermani.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These items were formerly stored in the "Charles X" cabinet in entry of Ashbery's New York City apartment.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oil on canvas, 20 x 20 inches.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Acrylic on canvas, 12 x 9 inches. Signed, dated, and titled on back.
This piece was displayed in Ashbery's New York City apartment living room on top of the bookcase used to house Ashbery's CD collection.

did.unittitle: Rosa Gallica Tontiana drawn by Pierre Jean Redoute Engraved by Poessin

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Unframed by Beinecke Library staff, November 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This mirror was a gift of Pierre Martory.

did.unittitle: Photograph of convex mirror in situ

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oil on masonite, 6 1/8 x 7 5/8 inches.

did.unittitle: Texts, images, objects from Ashbery's refrigerator

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photographs of the arrangement of the items on Ashbery's refrigerator.

did.unittitle: Ashbery's teacup, saucer, spoon, and tea box
David Kermani note, photograph of Ashbery's tea arrangements, and copy of Jill Krementz photograph of Ashbery with teacup

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The contents of Ashbery's desk drawer consists of small notebooks (including a diary from his 1989 trip to Japan), letters, postcards, business cards, address labels, fortune cookie fortunes, and assorted desk supplies.

did.unittitle: Photographs of desk items in situ by Emily Skillings

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These photographs were taken immediately prior to Ashbery's apartment being emptied on September 3, 2018.

did.unittitle: Artwork and objects from Ashbery's Hudson, New York house

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The artwork and objects from John Ashbery's Hudson, New York house are organized by room in sequence through the house. First floor rooms include the center hall, music room, downstairs library, dining room, butler's pantry, and kitchen. Second floor rooms include the second floor center hallway, art closet, second floor sitting room, second floor study, yellow room, and master bedroom. Other locations in the house represented in this series include the back staircase and the basement.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ashbery bought this ceramic number to display on the front of the Hudson house (39 West Court Street) shortly after purchasing the house at the end of 1978; it stayed on the house until it was listed for sale in 2020. (The house was sold in 2021.)

did.unittitle: Cane belonging to Ashbery's great-great uncle, Horatio Throop (1807-1888)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ashbery inherited this cane from his grandfather Henry Lawrence and kept it in the umbrella stand by the front door.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The first piece of Ashbery's ceramic collection. Found by David Kermani in the basement of their house in Hudson.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Brown with holes. Signed at bottom. Purchased by Ashbery.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Price sticker from 1973 reads "$38.00."

did.unittitle: Blue glass vase from dining room window

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ashbery inherited this tumbler from his grandmother, Addie Lawrence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ashbery received this doll as a present in 1989. He kept it in the dining room cabinet.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ashbery collected these match strikers in Paris beginning in the 1950s and later at Vito Giallo's antique shop in New York City.

did.unittitle: Four plates with images of fruits

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ashbery believed these plates were wedding gifts to his parents (his father owned a fruit farm), probably from a Francophile cousin of Henry Lawrence. The plates were made in St. Clement, France.

did.unittitle: Child's plate with text from the nursery rhyme "The House that Jack Built"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This plate was produced by a Roma manufacturer.

did.unittitle: Ovaltine mug featuring Little Orphan Annie and Sandy

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ashbery purchased this mug in an antique store to replace one he had as a child.

did.unittitle: Six piece Fiesta ware serving platter

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This tray was a gift to Ashbery from Kenneth Koch.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Purchased by Ashbery while travelling in Japan in 1989. Pocaro Sweat is a Japanese sports drink.

did.unittitle: Blue glass seltzer bottle from butler's pantry window

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One glass from a collection of eight.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This advertisement hung in the kitchen.

did.unittitle: Boy in Raincoat: Uneeda Biscuit Boy cup

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This cup was originally displayed on a shelf; the shelf broke and it was moved to the top of the refrigerator. Ashbery also had a framed print of the imaged which was popular in advertisements in the 1930s. Ashbery used the image in two collages: the 1970s untitled postcard collage of Garfield Park Conservatory in Chicago and "Popeye Steps Out - for Joe Brainard" (2016).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This dish set includes a cup, saucer, bowl, salad plate, and dinner plate.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The utensil set includes two forks, two spoons, and a knife.

did.unittitle: Rodgers Book Barn paper shopping bag

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Rodgers Book Barn in Hillsdale, New York, was one of Ashbery's favorite book stores.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Hand-colored screenprint, 26 x 19 3/4 inches, signed and dated.
This piece was a gift to Ashbery from the artist.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Unframed by Beinecke Library staff, November 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oil on masonite, 21 1/8 x 24 1.8.
Ashbery painted this piece at the Southampton home of his friend, the painter and poet Fairfield Porter.

"Mantlepiece" was included in the Literary Vision show at the Jack Tilton Gallery that ran from November 1-26, 1988, and which focused on visual artwork by writers. The exhibition catalogue pairs reproductions of visual art with short prose pieces by the writers, and includes a black and white reproduction of Ashbery's painting with an untitled prose text by him.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This box of postcards was in the art closet off the second floor center hall in Hudson. It was casually referred to — and labeled in the closet — as Ashbery's "favorites." These postcard images were ones he especially liked and several were used in collages. There are three types of postcards in this box and one miscellany group.

Found postcards: Ashbery and friends scoured antique and thrift shops for collections of used old postcards. There were two types, used postcards with wonderful images, which he kept in this box, and postcards with banal descriptions of travel, three favorites of which he placed on the mantel in the upstairs study.

Blank (mostly vintage) postcards: Most of the postcards in this box are blank and were purchased at antique shops or sometimes sent to Ashbery as presents from friends. There are a few with images that ended up in collages such as Caravaggio's Bacchus, which became Ashbery's Still Life (2016).

Postcards sent to Ashbery or that he wrote and never sent, including the following: 1) from Kenward [Elmslie] a letter with a found Hudson postcard March 14, [1984]; 2) A birthday postcard from Kenward [Elmslie] to Ashbery n.d. 3) A belated happy birthday from Kenward n.d. 4) from Larry [Ashmead] and Walter [Mathews] to Ashbery on his birthday n.d.; 5) from Anni [Lauterbach] on his birthday n.d.; 6) from Joe [Brainard] on Ashbery's birthday n.d. 7) signed "Anna Capri" that is from Ashbery to Harry Mathews and never sent [1962]; 8) Jan 6, 1998 #3 "Y N" postcard of Elizabeth Barrett Room, Roycroft Inn East Aurora NY to John Ashbery 49 West Court Street Hudson NY 12534.

Other: 1) a business card for Mary Martin and Bill Martin / 231 Rock Ridge Road / Millersville, MD 21108 "Antique postcards bought and sold" since 1964 (301) 647-7975 7); 2) postcard advertising the KATTERSKILL-KARDIAC POSTCARD CLUB; 3) Salon de Paris postcard partly cut out (an image which Ashbery later used for his collage "L'Auto Stop" [2015]); 4) Central New York Postcard Club; 5) receipt from 11-20-80 in Hudson 5) bookmark "Eastman's Wild Rose"; 6) Envelope with a 1990 Paris stamp commemorating 18 juin 1940 Appel a la resistance on back reads: "For John, flowers from Paris. Love, Loretta; 7) an envelope full of Ontario Steam-Boat Company tickets of various kinds, some stamped. The 1858 Independent Line Steamer Europa Capt. H.N. Throop, who was Ashbery's Pultneyville, NY ancestor [Horatio Throop's brother Washington built the house that Ashbery's grandfather Henry Lawrence inherited]; 8) piece of wrapping paper.

Many of these vintage postcards (both blank and used) are from locations that were important to Ashbery biographically. There are images from Rochester, Sodus, Pultneyville (the three locations where his family had homes and where he spent his childhood) and nearby locales such as Sodus Point, Sodus Bay and Newark (NY). There are also postcards from Deerfield (where Ashbery attended the last two years of high school at the Deerfield Academy); from Hudson (where Ashbery bought a home in 1978; Albany (where Ashbery's husband David Kermani grew up); NYC (where Ashbery rented apartments from 1949-55 and again from 1966-2017); and Los Angeles (where Ashbery liked to visit).

did.unittitle: Vest belonging to Henry Lawrence, Ashbery's maternal grandfather.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This vest was worn by Henry Lawrence while he was an undergraduate at the University of Rochester. Ashbery took it out of his closet in summer 2009 to look at it and it remained on a hanger on the sitting room door until 2018.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This tile was displayed on the sitting room mantel piece under "woman's wall". According to David Kermani it was one of Ashbery's "favorite things in the house."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This toothbrush holder is similar to one that Ashbery had as a child.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ink wash on paper, 17 x 22 1/2 inches. Signed and dated.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Unframed by Beinecke Library staff, November 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Mixed media on paper, 12 1/4 x 14 3/4 inches, signed. Framed, 20 3/4 x 23 1/4 inches.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This box was in the upstairs sitting room of the Hudson house on a coffee table for at least the last five years of Ashbery's life. He had plans to use some of these postcards in collages, and he also liked to look through them. Most of these "Carte Postales" are from Europe: cities in France, Italy, Germany, Belgium, Netherlands etc. Mixed into these place postcards is one that has a building carefully cut out, as though it had been intended for a collage.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This needle point was made for Ashbery by Martory. It was not made in 1956 when Ashbery won the Yale Younger Poets Prize for "Some Trees", but some time later when Martory took up needlepoint. It was on the armchair in Ashbery's study.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This item was originally a scarf or a pillow cover. Ashbery purchased it at an antique shop in the early 1980s and placed it on the second armchair in the study as a kind of antimacassar. It was torn and water stained when purchased.

did.unittitle: Parrot door knocker from Dartmouth Street house

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This doorknocker hung on the study door.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ashbery collected these materials for his collage work. They were kept at his collage-making stations in the house, including the second floor study.

did.unittitle: Board for Pollyanna, The Glad Game
National American Baseball Playing Field board game

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These scissors were a gift to Ashbery from Peter Hooten. Ashbery used them for his collage work.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ashbery received these collage materials from Joe Brainard over several decades. They include cut outs, postcards, and photocopies of original correspondence that accompanied the post cards. The original correspondence is in the Ashbery archive at Houghton Libary, Harvard University.

did.unittitle: Books and magazines used as collage materials

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These (mostly inexpensive, mostly art) books — the kind on the sale table at the Strand in New York City or found at the Book Barn in Hillsdale, NY (Ashbery's two favorite used book stores) — served as material for collages or as provocations for collage ideas. In about half of these books the specific pages that Ashbery planned to use are marked. During the last decade of Ashbery's life, artist and local Hudson resident Timothy O'Connor assisted Ashbery by scanning, marking, cutting out, and pasting materials. Ashbery would explain to O'Connor what he wanted done and O'Connor would carry out those instructions, checking in and sometimes working beside Ashbery. The post-its and handwritten notes in each book are in O'Connor's hand, except in one case where the note is by David Kermani. These books and magazines offer a kind of short-cut: a very quick, clear view of the kinds of images Ashbery chose to put in his collages.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: See, for example: p. 44-45 and John Ashbery's Fabergé Breakfast (2016).

did.unittitle: The Cambridge Library of Ornamental Art: Japanese Ornament from the 17th to 19th century (Gallery Books, 1991)
Italian Painting from Caravaggio to Modigliani (Albert Skira, 1952)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Pages marked:
1) p. 27 Caravaggio, Bacchus Ailing CA. 1590 [see Ashbery's collage "Still Life" (2016)
2) p. 39 Orazio Gentileschi Young Woman Playing the Lute CA. 1626
3) p. 69 Alessandro Magnasco Punchinello and His Son CA. 1720
3) p. 75 Giambattista Tiepolo Telemachus and Mentor CA. 1740

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Not marked but see Plate V "Venus with a Mirror" (1725) and John Ashbery's "Tiepolo Macaron" (2015)

did.unittitle: Fabbri, Fratelli, ed. Il Maestri del Colore: Vermeer (A. & G. Marco, 1964)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Pages not marked but see: VI-VII Veduta di Delft and Ashbery's poem "View of Delft" and XVII La merlettaia and Ashbery's collage.

did.unittitle: Christiansen, Keith, ed. Giambattista Tiepolo, 1696-1770 (The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York, 1996)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Pages not marked.

did.unittitle: Hornung, Clarence P. Handbook of Early Advertising Art (Dover Publications, 1956)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Pages not marked.

did.unittitle: Lands End Catalog addressed to Ashbery

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Page folded but nothing marked. For collages, see Ashbery's "Strawberry Stairs" (2015) and "Dark Decollation" (2015).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Marked: Tav. II La Deposizione di Christo
Note also Tav. XXIII
See Ashbery collages: "Salle d'Attente" (2016) and P.K. (2015) [and several others]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Marked: ("A Lady with a Dog" p. 98)
See John Ashbery's "Royal Family II" (2014)
See also cover of Bronzino (McCorquodale), see bottom right of Ashbery's "Mannerist Concerns" (2008)

did.unittitle: Gasser, Manuel, Angus Malcolm, trans. Self-Portraits from the Fifteen Century to the Present Day (Appleton-Centiury, 1961)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Pages marked for:
1) Parmigianino "Self-Portrait in a Convex Mirror" (1523) p. 60-61: see Ashbery's "Still Life" (2016) (above) and "The Mail in Norway" (2009)
2) Caravaggio "David with the Head of Goliath" (1605) p. 68-69 See Ashbery's Dark Decollation (2015)
3) Michael Sweerts "Self-Portrait" (1661) p. 96-97
4) Luis Meléndez "Self-Portrait" (1746) p. 112-113
5) Barthélemy Menn "Self-Portrait in a Straw Hat" (1872) p. 148-49
6) Pablo Picasso "Self-Portrait" (1906) p. 268-69

did.unittitle: Upton, Bertha. Florence K. Upton, illustrator. The Golliwogg's Desert Island (Longmans, Green & Co., 1906
Upton, Bertha. Florence K. Upton, illustrator. The Golliwogg's Circus (Longmans, Green & Co., 1903
Upton, Bertha. Florence K. Upton, illustrator. The Golliwogg's Polar Adventures (Longmans, Green & Co., 1903

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes note saying these are part of Ashbery's collage materials written by David Kermani.

did.unittitle: Sotheby's catalog, "Old Master & 19th Century European Art, New York 31 January 2009"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Marked: p. 86-87

did.unittitle: Petersham, Maud and Miska. The Story Book of Aircraft (The John C. Winston Company, 1935)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From Pittsford Elementary School Library.

did.unittitle: Olcott, Virginia, Constance Whittemore, illustrator. Jean and Fanchon, Children of Fair France (Silver Burdett and Company, 1931)
St. Nicholas: An Illustrated Magazine for Young Folks , vol. XI, part II, May 1884 to October 1884 (The Century Co., 1884)
Johnson, Virginia. Travels of an American Owl: A Satire (Claxton, Remsen & Haffelfinger, 1871)
Clarke, J. Erskine, ed. Chatterbox (Estes and Lauriat, 1886)
Practical Drawing: Arts and Crafts Course Part 2 (Practical Drawing Company, 1914)
Sotheby's catalog, "Photographic Images and Related Material, London, Friday 14th April 1989"
National Gallery catalog, "Giovanni Battista Moroni, 400th Anniversary Exhibition"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Advertisement for Canali marked by front cover fold over.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These collage materials include a Victorian board games book by Olivia Bristol, designs for Blackboard Drawing, Krazy Kat postcards, a signed postcard from Claire (Guillot) to Ashbery and Kermani, 2004, a signed postcard from Larry (Ashmead) to Ashbery, 1983, eight potential backdrops, marked magazine pages, a loot bag cut out, minature cut outs, ripped magazine pages, a Lands End catalog, and postcards.

did.unittitle: Print of "A Shepherd Boy" by Franz von Lenbach

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ashbery kept this print on the top shelf of the "yellow room" mantel. Ashbery kept a poster of the image from Schack Galeri, Munich (where the original is held), in the study closet. The image appears in Ashbery's collage "Promontory" (2010).

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Unframed by Beinecke Library staff, November 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This vase is a piece from Ashbery's end-of-day glass collection.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This vase is a piece from Ashbery's end-of-day glass collection. It was a gift to Ashbery from David Kermani.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: On board, unframed, 9 3/8 x 7 /12 inches.
Inscribed by Anne Dunn Moynihan, "To John from Anne, 24/10/65".

did.unittitle: Antique French paper puppet theater backdrop
Reproduction of antique French paper puppet theater backdrop

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: According to David Kermani, soon before his death in 2017, Ashbery selected this puppet theater backdrop as the background for his next collage. On August 31st an assistant hung it from a shelf of the bookcase facing his bed so he could study it. Ashbery passed on September 3rd, before beginning work on the collage.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Unframed by Beinecke Library staff, November 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ashbery purchased this poster in Paris in the late 1960s. It was originally a puppet theater background.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Unframed by Beinecke Library staff, November 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ashbery purchased this poster at the same shop in Paris as the Grand Theatre poster.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Unframed by Beinecke Library staff, November 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Pastel on paper, 12 x 9 inches.
Freilicher painted this pastel of Royal Anne cherries as a present for Ashbery.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Unframed by Beinecke Library staff, November 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This powder jar belonged to Helen Ashbery (John Ashbery's mother), and he particularly liked the sound it made when she put the lid on it. She kept face powder in it but he used it for odds and ends: a necklace, buttons, a fortune, a pair of scissors.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Signed, 10 1/4 x 8 1/4 inches (framed).
This painting depicts the small house built by Washington Throop and owned by John Ashbery's grandparents, Addie and Henry Lawrence, in Pultneyville, New York.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Unframed by Beinecke Library staff, November 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This print was a gift to Ashbery from Bill Berkson. Ashbery believed it to be by Maxfield Parrish. It is inscribed to Ashbery, "Happy Birthday John! 7.28.67."

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Unframed by Beinecke Library staff, November 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This print hung in the back stairwell. The image was Ashbery's screensaver.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Unframed by Beinecke Library staff, November 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This painting was a gift from David Kermani to Ashbery.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Unframed by Beinecke Library staff, November 2021.

did.unittitle: Framed poster of separate sheets of a 'Tom Thumb's Alphabet' by Edward Dalziel from an 1867 publication, The Child's Coloured Gift Book

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These bags were saved by Ashbery for their humorous/obscure text.
